title: Effective Java Complete Workshop - Merged single-file workshop covering Chapters 1-12

modules.name: 1 Introduction - Purpose and audience

questions.question: What audience does the book target?
options: Java developers who already know the language and want practical guidance for better design
Absolute beginners learning their first programming language
Only library authors working on the JDK itself
Only engineers writing concurrency-heavy systems
questions.explanation: The introduction says the book is not for beginners and assumes working knowledge of Java.

questions.question: How is the book structured?
options: Ninety independent items grouped into eleven chapters
Twelve chapters, each split into exactly eight items
A single continuous tutorial meant to be read in order
One chapter per Java release
questions.explanation: The text explicitly says the book consists of ninety items loosely grouped into eleven chapters.

questions.question: What is the recommended reading model?
options: Read items selectively and follow cross references as needed
Read strictly from the first page to the last page
Skip all code examples and focus on prose only
Treat the items as exhaustive formal proofs
questions.explanation: The introduction says each item stands on its own and the cross references help you plot your own course.

questions.question: What kind of examples does the book emphasize?
options: Code examples, including patterns and idioms that can be adapted directly
Only mathematical proofs of correctness
Only UML diagrams with no Java code
Only contrived toy examples with no production relevance
questions.explanation: The book says it is heavily illustrated with code examples and design patterns/idioms.

modules.name: 1 Introduction - Edition updates and release map

questions.question: Why does the book prefer release nicknames?
options: To make repeated version references easier to read
To avoid discussing any Java versions by number
To hide the fact that the book covers Java 8 and Java 9 features
To replace all technical terminology with informal language
questions.explanation: The introduction says nicknames are used in preference to official names for convenience.

questions.question: Which feature is explicitly associated with Java 9?
options: Modules
Streams
Lambdas
try-with-resources
questions.explanation: The release table maps modules to Java 9.

questions.question: What is the book's stance on terminology?
options: Mostly JLS-aligned, with a few pragmatic terms used consistently throughout
It invents a new vocabulary unrelated to the JLS
It avoids all formal terminology
It uses only vendor-specific terms from one IDE
questions.explanation: The introduction says most terms follow the JLS, but some are adjusted for clarity and convention.

questions.question: What does the book mean by an API?
options: The exported classes, interfaces, constructors, members, and serialized forms a programmer uses
Any internal helper method in a class
Only public static methods
Only a package's source files
questions.explanation: The introduction defines exported API and API elements explicitly.

modules.name: 1 Introduction - How to read the rules

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the examples in the book?
options: To illustrate design patterns, idioms, and mistakes with concrete Java code
To provide complete production-ready source files for reuse
To avoid any discussion of implementation details
To compare Java against unrelated languages
questions.explanation: The introduction says the book is heavily illustrated with code examples and anti-examples.

questions.question: What is the book's position on performance?
options: It is secondary to clarity, correctness, usability, robustness, flexibility, and maintainability
It is the only concern that matters
It is ignored entirely
It replaces all other design goals
questions.explanation: The introduction says the book is mostly not about performance.

questions.question: How should the rules be applied?
options: Generally, but not slavishly; violate them only occasionally and with good reason
Always, even when the rule conflicts with the problem
Never, because they are only historical notes
Only after consulting external style guides
questions.explanation: The introduction explicitly warns that the rules are best practices, not absolute laws.

questions.question: What is the book's definition of a component?
options: Any reusable software element, from a method to a multi-package framework
Only a Maven artifact
Only a class file
Only a user interface widget
questions.explanation: The introduction defines component broadly so the same rules can apply at different levels of reuse.

modules.name: 2 Creating and Destroying Objects - Construction APIs

questions.question: Which is the clearest reason to prefer a static factory over a constructor when the creation path needs a descriptive name?
options: The name can encode the construction semantics directly.
Constructors cannot return objects.
Constructors cannot be public.
Static factories are always faster than constructors.
questions.explanation: A static factory can communicate intent with a name such as `valueOf` or `of`, which is more readable than a bare constructor signature.

questions.question: Why can a static factory be more flexible than a constructor?
options: It can return any subtype of the declared return type.
It must always allocate a fresh object.
It cannot be overloaded.
It cannot hide implementation classes.
questions.explanation: The returned object may vary by subtype, by input, or by release, which is impossible with a constructor.

questions.question: What is the main failure mode of telescoping constructors for many optional parameters?
options: Callers must pass long positional lists of identically typed values.
They cannot be made public.
They force all fields to be mutable.
They cannot validate parameters.
questions.explanation: The core problem is readability and the risk of swapping same-typed arguments without compiler help.

questions.question: What is the main technical drawback of the JavaBeans pattern for object construction?
options: The object can be observed in an inconsistent intermediate state.
It prevents the use of setters.
It requires private constructors.
It forbids optional parameters.
questions.explanation: Construction is split across multiple calls, so invariants cannot be enforced atomically and immutability becomes awkward.

modules.name: 2 Creating and Destroying Objects - Controlled Instantiation

questions.question: What is the strongest modern choice for a singleton that also needs serialization and reflection resistance by default?
options: An enum singleton.
A public constructor.
An abstract class with one instance field.
A static utility class.
questions.explanation: An enum singleton gives the strongest built-in instance-control guarantee in Java.

questions.question: Why is making a utility class abstract not a valid way to prevent instantiation?
options: A subclass can still be instantiated.
The compiler removes all constructors.
Abstract classes cannot have static methods.
Abstract classes cannot be final.
questions.explanation: Abstract only blocks direct instantiation; it does not prevent subclass construction.

questions.question: What is the purpose of throwing `AssertionError` in a private utility-class constructor?
options: It prevents accidental construction even from inside the class.
It makes the class serializable.
It enables dependency injection.
It avoids the need for a private constructor.
questions.explanation: The exception is a defensive backstop in case the constructor is invoked by mistake during maintenance or reflection.

questions.question: What is the core benefit of dependency injection for resource-dependent classes?
options: It lets clients supply the dependency they actually need.
It forces the resource to be static.
It makes the class impossible to test.
It eliminates constructors.
questions.explanation: Injecting the resource gives the class flexibility, makes tests deterministic, and avoids hardwired global state.

questions.question: Why is `Supplier<? extends Tile>` a good input type for a factory dependency?
options: It allows the client to provide any factory that creates a Tile subtype.
It forces the factory to return `null` on failure.
It prevents lambdas from being used.
It requires the factory to be a singleton.
questions.explanation: The bounded wildcard preserves subtype flexibility while still expressing a factory contract.

modules.name: 2 Creating and Destroying Objects - Allocation Discipline

questions.question: Which allocation is most clearly unnecessary?
options: Creating `new String("bikini")` instead of reusing the string literal.
Creating a small immutable value object once.
Using a builder for a complex object.
Returning an interface from a factory.
questions.explanation: The string literal is already an object and can be reused directly.

questions.question: Why is caching a compiled `Pattern` often better than calling `String.matches` repeatedly?
options: The regular expression compilation is expensive and can be reused.
`String.matches` is not part of the JDK.
A `Pattern` cannot be immutable.
Caching only helps for boxed primitives.
questions.explanation: Repeatedly compiling the same regex creates avoidable work; a cached `Pattern` avoids that cost.

questions.question: What is the best general response when a custom stack or array-backed container removes an element?
options: Null out the now-obsolete slot.
Leave the reference in place for debugging.
Clone the entire array on every pop.
Convert the array to a `WeakHashMap`.
questions.explanation: The slot is no longer part of the active set, so clearing it lets the GC reclaim the object graph behind it.

questions.question: When is `WeakHashMap` the right cache implementation?
options: When entry lifetime should follow external references to the key.
When entries must stay forever.
When all values are primitives.
When the cache is only used once.
questions.explanation: Weak references are useful only when the cache entry is relevant for as long as the key is externally reachable.

modules.name: 2 Creating and Destroying Objects - Cleanup and Resource Safety

questions.question: Why are finalizers and cleaners poor choices for ordinary resource management?
options: They are not guaranteed to run promptly or at all.
They are required by every closeable type.
They run only on the main thread.
They cannot be used with native resources.
questions.explanation: The timing and even execution of cleanup are not reliable enough for correctness or resource limits.

questions.question: What is the best modern pattern for a class that owns a resource requiring explicit termination?
options: Implement `AutoCloseable` and require clients to call `close()`.
Rely on a finalizer as the primary cleanup path.
Make the resource static.
Hide the resource inside a singleton.
questions.explanation: The class should expose explicit closure and, if desired, keep a cleaner only as a fallback safety net.

questions.question: What happens when both the body of a try-finally block and the `close()` call throw?
options: The later exception can obscure the earlier one.
Both exceptions are always printed equally.
The compiler rewrites it as try-with-resources.
The close exception is ignored by definition.
questions.explanation: Old try-finally code often loses the primary failure, which is one of the reasons try-with-resources was introduced.

questions.question: Which interface must a resource implement to be usable in try-with-resources?
options: `AutoCloseable`.
`Serializable`.
`CloseableFactory`.
`Runnable`.
questions.explanation: `AutoCloseable` is the contract that gives the compiler a resource to close automatically.

questions.question: Can a try-with-resources statement also have a catch clause?
options: Yes, it can handle exceptions without extra nesting.
No, the syntax forbids it.
Only if the resource is finalizable.
Only inside a loop.
questions.explanation: A catch clause may follow the resource specification exactly as it can follow a try-finally statement.

modules.name: 3 Methods Common to All Objects - Equality and hash codes

questions.question: When is it reasonable to leave equals inherited from Object?
options: When each instance is inherently unique or the class has no logical equality concept
Whenever the class has many fields
Only when the class is final and public
Only when the class also overrides hashCode
questions.explanation: Object identity is the right semantics for active entities and for classes that do not need logical equality.

questions.question: Which equals property is violated by one-way comparisons against String?
options: Symmetry
Reflexivity
Consistency
Null handling
questions.explanation: If cis.equals(s) can be true while s.equals(cis) is false, the two objects do not agree on equality.

questions.question: Why does a getClass-based equals method often fail for subclasses?
options: It prevents a subtype from being equal to a valid supertype view of the same value
It makes hash codes slower to compute
It violates reflexivity for final classes
It forces every object to be mutable
questions.explanation: A subclass is still an instance of the supertype, but getClass equality rejects that relationship outright.

questions.question: What is the main problem with a constant hashCode such as 42?
options: It is legal but collapses all entries into one bucket and destroys hash table performance
It makes equal objects unequal
It breaks reflexivity
It prevents compilation on modern JDKs
questions.explanation: The contract allows collisions, but one bucket for everything is effectively a denial of service for hash tables.

questions.question: Which fields should participate in hashCode?
options: Only the significant fields used by equals
Every field, including transient and derived fields
Only primitive fields
Only fields declared final
questions.explanation: hashCode must mirror equals, so it should be built from the same state that determines logical equality.

modules.name: 3 Methods Common to All Objects - String form and copying

questions.question: What should a good toString method provide?
options: A concise but informative representation that a person can read
The exact hash code in hexadecimal
A full binary dump of every field
A string that cannot be parsed or logged
questions.explanation: The contract explicitly favors human readability and useful diagnostics.

questions.question: When is it most useful to document the exact toString format?
options: For value classes where the string may be used as stable human-readable input and output
Only for private helper classes
Only when the class is mutable
Never, because formats should always be hidden
questions.explanation: A precise format is helpful when the string representation is intended to be a standard representation.

questions.question: What is the required first step in a well-behaved clone implementation?
options: Call super.clone
Allocate the copy with new
Serialize the object to bytes
Throw CloneNotSupportedException immediately
questions.explanation: The Object contract and the chapter both emphasize super.clone as the field-by-field base copy.

questions.question: Why can a shallow clone be dangerous for mutable internals?
options: The clone and the original may share mutable subobjects
It always violates equals
It makes the object non-final
It removes all constructors
questions.explanation: If both objects point at the same mutable array or list, changes in one leak into the other.

questions.question: What is the preferred copy mechanism for most new classes?
options: A copy constructor or copy factory
A public clone method
A protected finalize method
A custom readObject method
questions.explanation: The chapter treats constructors and factories as simpler, safer, and more flexible than Cloneable.

modules.name: 3 Methods Common to All Objects - Comparable and ordering

questions.question: What does implementing Comparable advertise about a class?
options: That its instances have a natural ordering
That its instances are immutable
That its instances can be cloned safely
That its instances always have unique hash codes
questions.explanation: Comparable is the marker for a class whose instances can be ordered without an external comparator.

questions.question: Why should you avoid subtracting one field value from another in compareTo?
options: Overflow and floating point edge cases can break the ordering
It cannot compile for any primitive type
It always returns zero
It makes the method abstract
questions.explanation: The chapter calls difference-based comparison broken because arithmetic overflow can reverse the sign.

questions.question: Which statement about compareTo and equals is correct?
options: Consistency with equals is recommended but not required
They must always return the same result for every pair of objects
compareTo must never throw ClassCastException
equals is ignored by all sorted collections
questions.explanation: The contract allows inconsistency, but the class should document it clearly when it exists.

questions.question: What do TreeSet and TreeMap use to decide element equality?
options: The ordering imposed by compareTo
Only object identity
Only hashCode
The finalize method
questions.explanation: Sorted collections rely on ordering, so compareTo takes the role that equals plays in hash-based collections.

questions.question: Which helper is preferred for primitive comparisons inside compareTo?
options: The static compare methods such as Integer.compare and Short.compare
The minus operator
The instanceof operator
The clone method
questions.explanation: Static compare helpers are clearer, safer, and less error-prone than subtraction.

modules.name: 4 Classes and Interfaces - Encapsulation and public API shape

questions.question: For a public class, what is the preferred access level for a helper type that is only used inside the package?
options: package-private
public
protected
private
questions.explanation: Package-private keeps the type usable inside the package without exporting it as part of the public API.

questions.question: Why are public mutable fields discouraged in exported classes?
options: They freeze representation into the API and prevent invariant enforcement
They make the class abstract
They speed up serialization automatically
They improve binary compatibility
questions.explanation: A public mutable field exposes implementation details and removes the class's ability to control updates.

questions.question: What is wrong with a public static final array field?
options: Its reference is final, but the array contents remain mutable
Arrays cannot be final
The compiler rejects all array constants
It cannot be read from other classes
questions.explanation: The array object itself can still be modified through the returned reference.

questions.question: Which statement about protected members of exported classes is correct?
options: They are part of the exported API and should be rare
They are invisible outside the package
They are always safer than private members
They are only relevant to interfaces
questions.explanation: Protected members are a long-term compatibility promise, so they should be used sparingly.

modules.name: 4 Classes and Interfaces - Immutability and composition

questions.question: Which rule is part of making a class immutable?
options: Make all fields private and final, and do not expose mutable internals
Make every method static
Avoid constructors entirely
Use public setters for all fields
questions.explanation: Immutability requires fixed state and no external mutation channel.

questions.question: Why are immutable objects attractive for concurrent code?
options: They are inherently thread-safe and need no synchronization
They cannot be serialized
They always allocate less memory
They eliminate the need for equals
questions.explanation: If state never changes, concurrent readers cannot observe a race on that state.

questions.question: What is the main risk of implementation inheritance across package boundaries?
options: The subclass can break when superclass internals change
The subclass cannot compile at all
The JVM forbids it
It automatically duplicates all methods
questions.explanation: A subclass can depend on superclass behavior that was never part of the public contract.

questions.question: What is the best general replacement for fragile subclassing of a concrete class?
options: Composition plus forwarding methods
A public tag field
More protected methods
A larger abstract superclass
questions.explanation: Composition lets you control the exposed API and avoids tight coupling to internals.

modules.name: 4 Classes and Interfaces - Inheritance contracts and interface design

questions.question: What must a class document if it is designed for inheritance?
options: Its self-use of overridable methods and the effects of overriding them
Only its package name
Only its private fields
Nothing, because inheritance is self-documenting
questions.explanation: Subclass authors need to know exactly which overridable methods the superclass calls and when.

questions.question: Why are default methods risky when added to an existing interface?
options: They can silently break preexisting implementations at runtime
They cannot be compiled by javac
They are always ignored by clients
They forbid all abstract methods
questions.explanation: Existing classes may inherit behavior that does not preserve their invariants.

questions.question: Which is the best use of an interface according to Item 22?
options: To define a type that clients can use to refer to instances
To export constants without qualification
To store instance fields
To replace every utility class
questions.explanation: Interfaces should describe what a type is, not merely provide a bag of constants.

questions.question: What is a skeletal implementation class for?
options: It supplies reusable method bodies while leaving the type as an interface
It prevents all subclassing
It replaces every default method
It is only for constants
questions.explanation: Skeletal implementations reduce the work of implementing an interface without forcing inheritance for clients.

modules.name: 4 Classes and Interfaces - Tagged classes, member classes, and source files

questions.question: Why are tagged classes discouraged?
options: They force irrelevant fields and switch logic into one brittle type
They cannot be instantiated
They are illegal in Java
They always use abstract methods
questions.explanation: A tagged class mixes multiple flavors in one type, which is verbose and error-prone.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of a nonstatic member class?
options: It stores an implicit reference to its enclosing instance
It cannot access private fields
It is always abstract
It cannot be serialized
questions.explanation: That enclosing-instance reference consumes space and can extend object lifetime unintentionally.

questions.question: When is an anonymous class appropriate?
options: For a short one-off use where naming the type is unnecessary
For implementing multiple interfaces at once
For defining a public API class
For replacing every nested class
questions.explanation: Anonymous classes are limited and should stay small; lambdas are usually preferred for function objects.

questions.question: What is the rule for top-level source files in this chapter?
options: Limit each source file to one top-level class or interface
Put all utility classes in one file
Use multiple files only for public classes
Keep every nested type top-level
questions.explanation: One top-level type per file avoids duplicate-definition and order-dependent compiler behavior.

modules.name: 5 Generics - Raw Types and Unchecked Warnings

questions.question: Why is `List<?>` preferable to a raw `List` when the element type is unknown?
options: It preserves compile-time type safety while still accepting any element type.
It allows inserting any object into the list without warnings.
It erases fewer runtime checks than a raw list.
It makes the list reifiable at runtime.
questions.explanation: A wildcard expresses unknown type information without discarding generic checking; a raw type discards checking entirely.

questions.question: What should you do first when you see an unchecked warning?
options: Try to eliminate the warning by fixing the declaration or implementation.
Add `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` to the class.
Ignore it because the compiler already accepted the code.
Convert the code to use raw types so the warning disappears.
questions.explanation: Unchecked warnings are defects until you can prove the code is actually safe.

questions.question: What is the danger of using a raw collection type in client code?
options: It can let an invalid element through and defer the failure to runtime.
It automatically makes the collection immutable.
It prevents the collection from compiling with any methods.
It forces all elements to become `Object` references at runtime.
questions.explanation: Raw types suppress generic checking, so a bad insertion can surface later as a `ClassCastException`.

questions.question: Where should `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` normally be placed?
options: On the smallest declaration or statement scope that you can prove is safe.
On the entire package so all related warnings are silenced.
On the whole class to keep the source file clean.
On every method that uses generics.
questions.explanation: The book’s rule is narrow scope only; broad suppression hides new problems.

questions.question: Why are raw types retained in the language at all?
options: To preserve binary and source compatibility with pre-generics code.
Because generic types cannot interoperate with old libraries.
Because the compiler requires raw types for all inheritance hierarchies.
Because raw types are more efficient than parameterized types.
questions.explanation: Raw types are a compatibility escape hatch, not a recommended style.

modules.name: 5 Generics - Generic Types and Generic Methods

questions.question: Why is `public static <E> Set<E> union(Set<E> s1, Set<E> s2)` better than a raw version?
options: It preserves the element type and eliminates unchecked warnings for callers.
It allows any set type to be passed without compile-time checks.
It removes the need for a return type.
It makes the method usable only with `Set<Object>`.
questions.explanation: A generic method ties the inputs and output to a single type parameter.

questions.question: What does `<E extends Comparable<E>>` mean in practice?
options: Values of `E` can be compared to other values of the same `E`.
Values of `E` can be compared to any `Object`.
Values of `E` must be arrays.
Values of `E` must implement `Serializable`.
questions.explanation: This recursive bound captures the usual self-comparable case used by `max`.

questions.question: When generifying a class backed by an array, what is the usual safe pattern?
options: Store an `Object[]`, cast once to `E[]`, and prove the cast is safe locally.
Create a `new E[]` array and suppress the compiler error.
Use a raw `List` internally to avoid warnings.
Expose the backing array so callers can manage typing.
questions.explanation: The book’s preferred approach is a narrow, justified unchecked cast plus local suppression.

questions.question: Why are generic methods important even when the class itself is not generic?
options: They let utility APIs preserve type relationships without forcing casts.
They make every class automatically thread-safe.
They disable type inference so callers must specify everything explicitly.
They are only useful for collections libraries.
questions.explanation: Many algorithms are naturally method-level generic, not class-level generic.

questions.question: What is the benefit of a recursive type bound over an unchecked cast?
options: The compiler can verify the intended ordering constraint at compile time.
It makes the type non-reifiable.
It allows raw types to compile faster.
It turns runtime errors into warnings.
questions.explanation: A recursive bound encodes the contract directly instead of relying on comments or casts.

modules.name: 5 Generics - Lists, Arrays, and Wildcards

questions.question: Why is `List<String>` not a subtype of `List<Object>`?
options: Because generic types are invariant.
Because `String` is not a subtype of `Object`.
Because lists are reified at runtime.
Because wildcards are required only for arrays.
questions.explanation: Generics do not inherit subtype relationships the way arrays do.

questions.question: What is the most important difference between arrays and generics?
options: Arrays are reified and checked at runtime; generics are erased and checked at compile time.
Arrays are immutable; generics are mutable.
Generics support covariance; arrays do not.
Arrays never throw runtime exceptions.
questions.explanation: The runtime type information that arrays keep is exactly why they behave differently.

questions.question: What does PECS stand for?
options: Producer-extends, consumer-super.
Parameter-equals-class structure.
Public APIs need capture-safe signatures.
Prefer erased collections over specialized collections.
questions.explanation: It is the book’s mnemonic for choosing bounded wildcards.

questions.question: Why should return types generally not use bounded wildcards?
options: They force callers to deal with wildcards instead of giving them a precise result type.
They make the method less generic.
They disable type inference.
They always require a raw cast internally.
questions.explanation: Wildcards are for input flexibility; return types should be as specific as possible.

questions.question: What is wildcard capture used for?
options: To let a private helper recover the exact type hidden behind `?`.
To convert any raw type into a parameterized type automatically.
To make arrays reified.
To eliminate the need for type parameters.
questions.explanation: Capture lets implementation code regain precision without exposing it in the public API.

modules.name: 5 Generics - Varargs and Heterogeneous Containers

questions.question: What is heap pollution in the context of generic varargs?
options: A parameterized type reference points to an object that is not actually of that type.
Any array created by the JVM at runtime.
A warning emitted whenever `List` is used.
A situation where `@SafeVarargs` is missing.
questions.explanation: Heap pollution is the mismatch between the variable’s declared generic type and the object it refers to.

questions.question: When is `@SafeVarargs` appropriate?
options: Only when the method cannot be overridden and you have proven the varargs usage is safe.
On any method that takes at least one array parameter.
On every generic method automatically.
Only on methods that return arrays.
questions.explanation: The annotation is a trust statement about a specific generic-varargs implementation.

questions.question: Why is `List` often a better API shape than a generic varargs parameter?
options: It avoids the array-backed varargs mechanism and the related heap-pollution warnings.
It makes the method reifiable.
It always performs better than arrays.
It removes the need for generics entirely.
questions.explanation: A list parameter is often the simplest way to keep the API typesafe and warning-free.

questions.question: What is the key idea behind the `Favorites` pattern?
options: Use a parameterized key, usually `Class<T>`, to store and retrieve values of many types safely.
Store everything in a raw `Map` and cast at every call site.
Use arrays so the runtime can enforce the key type.
Use wildcard return types to maximize flexibility.
questions.explanation: The container stays heterogeneous, while each access remains typechecked through the key.

questions.question: Why can `Favorites` safely call `type.cast(...)` on retrieval?
options: Because `Class.cast` performs a checked dynamic cast tied to the key’s runtime type.
Because `cast` is just syntactic sugar for a raw cast.
Because the map stores only primitive values.
Because all `Class` objects are reifiable wildcards.
questions.explanation: The cast reestablishes the key/value type linkage at the point of use.

modules.name: 6 Enums and Annotations - Enums are real types

questions.question: Why is the int-enum pattern inferior to a real enum type?
options: It provides type safety, a namespace, and stable binaries for clients.
It is faster than enums in every case.
It supports inheritance better than enums.
It automatically gives you set operations.
questions.explanation: Int constants are brittle: they can be mixed accidentally, collide by name, and are compiled into client code. Real enums are type-safe classes.

questions.question: What is the main reason to prefer instance fields over ordinal() for associated data?
options: Ordinal changes when constants are reordered; a stored field does not.
Ordinal is always slower than a stored field.
Ordinal cannot be called on enums with methods.
Stored fields cannot be final.
questions.explanation: An enum's ordinal is only the declaration position, so it is not a semantic value and must not drive program logic.

questions.question: When should you use a switch on an enum?
options: When you are augmenting an enum you do not control, or the logic does not belong in the enum itself.
Whenever you want compile-time checking for missing constants.
Whenever the enum has instance fields.
Whenever you need constant-specific behavior.
questions.explanation: A switch is appropriate as a client-side adapter, not as the primary way to model per-constant behavior inside the enum.

questions.question: What does an enum constructor give you that int constants cannot?
options: A place to bind per-constant data and compute derived state once.
A way to create multiple instances per constant.
Access to mutable static fields before initialization.
A namespace shared across unrelated enums.
questions.explanation: Each enum constant can carry its own state and behavior, which is impossible with bare ints.

modules.name: 6 Enums and Annotations - EnumSet and EnumMap

questions.question: Why is EnumSet preferred over a manual bit field?
options: It preserves Set semantics and type safety while keeping bit-vector performance.
It uses more memory than any other Set implementation.
It can represent sets with more than 64 elements in one long.
It is immutable by default.
questions.explanation: EnumSet gives you compact representation and normal Set operations without manual bit masks.

questions.question: Why should public APIs take Set<Style> instead of EnumSet<Style>?
options: So callers are not forced to use one specific implementation.
Because EnumSet cannot be passed to methods.
Because Set is always faster than EnumSet.
Because EnumSet is not a valid parameter type.
questions.explanation: Program to the interface. Accepting Set keeps the API flexible even if EnumSet is the best implementation choice.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of EnumMap over ordinal-indexed arrays?
options: It is type-safe and self-describing while staying close to array speed.
It supports non-enum keys better than HashMap.
It avoids the need for a key type token.
It is automatically immutable.
questions.explanation: EnumMap hides the ordinal arithmetic and still uses array-backed storage internally.

questions.question: When should you specify a map factory with groupingBy?
options: When you care that the collector produce EnumMap rather than an implementation chosen by the library.
Whenever the stream has more than one element.
Only when collecting to a Set.
Only when the source is already a map.
questions.explanation: The default groupingBy collector may choose a different Map implementation; provide a factory if EnumMap behavior matters.

modules.name: 6 Enums and Annotations - Extensible enums and annotations

questions.question: Why are annotations better than naming patterns for framework hooks?
options: They are checked by the compiler and can carry structured metadata.
They execute faster at runtime.
They automatically fix invalid code.
They are only available for methods.
questions.explanation: Annotations avoid silent typos and let tools inspect explicit metadata instead of parsing names.

questions.question: What does @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) do for a test annotation?
options: It keeps the annotation visible to reflection at runtime.
It makes the annotation compile faster.
It allows the annotation only on types.
It forces the annotation to be repeatable.
questions.explanation: Without runtime retention, a reflection-based test runner cannot see the annotation.

questions.question: What is the main point of a bounded type token like Class<? extends Throwable>?
options: It lets the annotation accept only class literals for throwable subtypes.
It forces the value to be a String at runtime.
It makes annotation processing unnecessary.
It allows any unrelated class to be passed in.
questions.explanation: The wildcard constrains the class literal to a Throwable subtype and keeps the API type-safe.

questions.question: Why can repeated annotations be easy to process incorrectly?
options: Because the repeated form is represented through a synthetic container annotation.
Because Java forbids runtime reflection on annotations.
Because repeated annotations cannot carry parameters.
Because repeatable annotations are not retained.
questions.explanation: A processor must look for the direct annotation and its container, or use getAnnotationsByType.

modules.name: 6 Enums and Annotations - Override and marker interfaces

questions.question: What bug does @Override most directly prevent?
options: Accidentally overloading a method when you intended to override it.
Using final fields in enums.
Missing import statements for superclasses.
Unchecked generic casts.
questions.explanation: It forces the compiler to confirm that the signature actually matches a supertype method.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a marker interface over a marker annotation?
options: It creates a real type that APIs can require in method signatures.
It can be applied to methods and fields.
It is always shorter to write.
It does not require any inheritance relationship.
questions.explanation: A marker interface can be used for compile-time type checking and subtype restriction.

questions.question: When is a marker annotation the better choice?
options: When the marker applies to non-type elements or fits an annotation-driven framework.
Whenever you need subtype checking at compile time.
Whenever the marker should be inherited by default.
Whenever the marker adds methods.
questions.explanation: Annotations are the right fit for metadata on methods, fields, parameters, and framework hooks.

questions.question: Why is Serializable a marker interface rather than a marker annotation?
options: Because serialization is a capability that is naturally modeled as a type.
Because annotations did not exist when serialization was invented.
Because marker annotations cannot have runtime retention.
Because interfaces cannot be checked by the compiler.
questions.explanation: The interface lets APIs express and enforce a type-level contract for serializability.

modules.name: 7 Lambdas and Streams - Lambdas, anonymous classes, and method references

questions.question: When is a lambda usually preferable to an anonymous class?
options: When you need a concise implementation of a functional interface and do not need a separate named type
When you need to override `equals` and `hashCode`
When you want the class to be reusable through inheritance
When the callback must access private members of any object
questions.explanation: Lambdas are the preferred syntax for function objects; anonymous classes are still useful when you need a distinct class body.

questions.question: What is the main reason to extract a long lambda into a method?
options: It improves readability by giving the behavior a real name and documentation site
It makes the code parallel by default
It guarantees allocation-free execution
It changes the lambda into a singleton
questions.explanation: The book favors small lambdas; complex behavior should move into named methods.

questions.question: What is the normal advantage of a method reference over the equivalent lambda?
options: It is usually shorter and clearer when the lambda only forwards to an existing method
It can express more behavior than a lambda
It avoids type inference
It works only for static methods
questions.explanation: Method references are a shorthand, not a new capability.

questions.question: Why is `Integer::sum` better than `(count, incr) -> count + incr` in some contexts?
options: It removes boilerplate without losing meaning
It is the only legal syntax for `merge`
It avoids boxing overhead entirely
It makes the lambda stateful
questions.explanation: Use the method reference when the intent is obviously simple forwarding to a known method.

questions.question: What should make you hesitate before replacing a lambda with a method reference?
options: The lambda parameter names carry useful semantic documentation
The target type is a functional interface
The method reference compiles
The code is inside a stream pipeline
questions.explanation: A lambda can be clearer when naming the parameters explains the computation better than the method name does.

modules.name: 7 Lambdas and Streams - Functional interfaces and stream fundamentals

questions.question: When should you prefer a standard functional interface over a custom one?
options: When the standard type matches the required signature and semantics
Only when the API is internal
Only if the interface has exactly two abstract methods
Only when you need generics
questions.explanation: The standard interfaces are shared vocabulary; custom interfaces are for genuinely new contracts.

questions.question: Why should a custom functional interface be annotated with `@FunctionalInterface`?
options: It enforces the single-abstract-method contract and documents intent
It makes the interface serializable
It guarantees lambda performance
It prevents the interface from being public
questions.explanation: The annotation is a compile-time guard and an API signal.

questions.question: What is the key evaluation rule for a stream pipeline?
options: Nothing runs until the terminal operation is invoked
Every stage runs immediately when it is declared
Only the source stage is lazy
Intermediate operations always materialize collections
questions.explanation: Laziness is what makes stream pipelines compositional and sometimes able to handle infinite sources.

questions.question: Why does Item 45 warn that streams are not a universal replacement for iteration?
options: Because some problems are clearer or simpler with loops, and some APIs still need iterable results
Because stream operations cannot filter data
Because streams are slower by definition
Because streams cannot be used on collections
questions.explanation: The book treats streams as a tool with a strong fit for bulk transformations, not a mandate.

questions.question: What is a terminal operation doing in a stream pipeline?
options: It forces evaluation and produces the final result
It only renames the pipeline
It converts the stream back to an array automatically
It makes all intermediate operations parallel
questions.explanation: Without a terminal operation, a stream pipeline is a no-op.

modules.name: 7 Lambdas and Streams - Side effects, return types, and parallelism

questions.question: What is wrong with using `forEach` to build a frequency table by mutating an external map?
options: It turns the pipeline into imperative code with side effects instead of a pure stream computation
It prevents compilation unless the map is immutable
It always executes in parallel
It cannot read lowercase words
questions.explanation: Item 46 says the stream should compute the result, not merely drive mutation in a terminal action.

questions.question: Which collector family best matches the idea of building results without side effects?
options: Collectors such as `groupingBy`, `counting`, `toList`, `toSet`, and `toMap`
Only `forEach` and `peek`
Only `parallel()`
Only `reduce` with mutable accumulators
questions.explanation: Collectors are the intended mutable-reduction mechanism for stream pipelines.

questions.question: What is the preferred return type when a method can naturally materialize its results and clients may want to iterate?
options: A standard collection such as `ArrayList`, `Set`, or `Collection`
Always `Stream`
Always `Iterable`
Always an array
questions.explanation: Returning a collection supports both iteration and streaming and is usually the least surprising API.

questions.question: When is `parallel()` most promising?
options: When the source splits cheaply and the computation is substantial and well-structured
Whenever the pipeline contains `filter`
Whenever the stream is infinite
Whenever the code is short
questions.explanation: Parallel stream performance depends on splittability, locality, and terminal-operation behavior.

questions.question: Why does Item 48 insist on measurements before enabling parallel streams in production?
options: Because parallelism can regress correctness or performance, and the effect is workload-specific
Because `parallel()` is deprecated
Because the JVM never uses more than one core
Because streams cannot run in parallel on collections
questions.explanation: The book treats parallelism as a proof obligation, not a guess.

modules.name: 8 Methods - Parameter validity and object boundaries

questions.question: Why should a public method validate bad parameters at entry?
options: So the method fails fast, preserves object invariants, and reports the documented exception.
So the method can avoid all documentation for its parameters.
So the method can always continue with a default value.
So the caller can inspect the object state after the failure.
questions.explanation: Parameter checks are about contract enforcement and failure atomicity, not just defensive programming.

questions.question: When is a separate validity check not worth doing?
options: When the main computation will naturally validate the inputs and the extra check would be redundant or expensive.
Whenever the method is public.
Whenever the method returns void.
Whenever the input type is an interface.
questions.explanation: The item allows implicit validation, but only if you understand the trade-off and the resulting exception behavior.

questions.question: Why should a constructor copy a mutable argument before storing it?
options: To prevent later caller mutations from corrupting the object's invariants.
To make the field final.
To avoid the need for Javadoc.
To allow the object to share state intentionally.
questions.explanation: Storing the caller's object creates an alias into your representation; a defensive copy breaks that alias.

questions.question: Why do defensive copies on accessors matter even for immutable-looking classes?
options: Because returning a mutable internal reference hands callers a back door into the object's state.
Because accessors are slower than constructors.
Because `Date` cannot be returned from a method.
Because the JVM automatically copies every return value anyway.
questions.explanation: Returning a live mutable object exposes the representation; returning a copy preserves encapsulation.

modules.name: 8 Methods - Signature shape and overloads

questions.question: Why are long parameter lists a smell?
options: They are hard to remember, easy to transpose, and difficult to use without constant reference to docs.
Because Java forbids methods with more than four parameters.
Because they always allocate arrays at runtime.
Because they prevent JIT inlining.
questions.explanation: The practical issue is API usability, not language restriction.

questions.question: Why prefer an interface type over a class type in a parameter list?
options: It keeps the API flexible and avoids forcing callers into a particular implementation.
It makes the method more abstract and slower.
It guarantees immutability.
It lets the compiler skip null checks.
questions.explanation: Programming to the interface avoids unnecessary copying and needless coupling.

questions.question: Why is overloading with the same arity risky?
options: Because the compiler chooses based on compile-time types, so the obvious-looking call can bind to the wrong method.
Because the JVM randomly picks one overload at runtime.
Because overloaded methods cannot be public.
Because autoboxing eliminates all ambiguity.
questions.explanation: This is the core lesson of `CollectionClassifier` and the `remove(int)` versus `remove(Integer)` problem.

questions.question: When is overload ambiguity least likely to confuse users?
options: When the parameter types are radically different and no actual argument can plausibly fit both signatures.
Whenever the methods have the same name.
Whenever one overload is static.
Whenever the return types differ.
questions.explanation: Radically different types make the choice obvious; otherwise, prefer different method names.

modules.name: 8 Methods - Varargs and absence

questions.question: Why does every varargs invocation have a cost?
options: Because the compiler creates and initializes an array for the arguments at the call site.
Because varargs force reflection.
Because varargs disable inlining.
Because the JVM boxes every argument twice.
questions.explanation: The flexibility is convenient, but it still compiles to array creation.

questions.question: What is the correct pattern for a method that needs at least one value and then any number more?
options: Take one normal argument first, then a trailing varargs parameter.
Take only a varargs parameter and check `args.length` at runtime.
Take an `Optional<T>` and a varargs parameter together.
Overload the method with a separate empty signature.
questions.explanation: The leading required parameter makes the contract compile-time explicit.

questions.question: Why should an API return an empty collection or array instead of null?
options: It removes special-case client code and avoids null handling without real performance gain.
Because empty containers are always allocated on the stack.
Because null is illegal as a return value.
Because callers cannot test for null in Java.
questions.explanation: Returning null for absence pushes avoidable complexity into every caller.

questions.question: When is `Optional<T>` the right return type?
options: When the method may not be able to return a value and the caller should be forced to think about that possibility.
Whenever a method returns a collection.
Whenever a field is optional.
Whenever you want to avoid exceptions entirely.
questions.explanation: Optional is a return-value tool, not a general replacement for null everywhere.

modules.name: 8 Methods - Documentation

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a doc comment on an exported API element?
options: To specify the contract a client relies on, including preconditions, postconditions, and side effects.
To restate the implementation line by line.
To replace code review.
To satisfy the compiler.
questions.explanation: Documentation is part of the API, not a paraphrase of the source code.

questions.question: Which tags are expected for a normal method contract?
options: `@param`, `@return`, and `@throws`.
`@implSpec`, `@index`, and `@literal`.
`@override`, `@serial`, and `@code`.
Only `@return`.
questions.explanation: Those three tags are the standard way to document parameters, results, and exception conditions.

questions.question: When should `{@implSpec}` be used?
options: When documenting the self-use contract for subclasses or overriding code.
Whenever a method is public.
Whenever the method is generic.
Whenever you want to hide implementation details from callers.
questions.explanation: It is for inheritance contracts, not for client-facing behavior descriptions.

questions.question: What should you document for generic types, enums, annotations, and packages?
options: Type parameters, constants, annotation members, and package/module-level comments where appropriate.
Only the public constructors.
Only the top-level class summary.
Nothing extra, because Javadoc infers it.
questions.explanation: Those elements have API meaning and need explicit documentation to stay usable.

modules.name: 9 General Programming - Local Scope and for-each

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of declaring a local variable at first use?
options: It minimizes scope and reduces clutter and accidental misuse.
It avoids the need for an initializer.
It makes the variable visible to later methods.
It guarantees the variable is final.
questions.explanation: Narrow scope improves readability and reduces the chance that a variable is observed outside its intended region.

questions.question: When is a traditional for loop justified over for-each?
options: When you need the iterator, index, removal, replacement, or parallel traversal control.
Whenever the container is a collection rather than an array.
Whenever performance matters.
Whenever the body contains more than one statement.
questions.explanation: For-each is the default; explicit control is for the few cases where the iterator or index is semantically required.

questions.question: Why are while loops more error-prone for simple iteration?
options: They leave the loop variable in scope longer and make copy-and-paste mistakes harder to catch.
They are slower than for loops.
They cannot iterate over arrays.
They require recursion behind the scenes.
questions.explanation: The book's concern is scope and clarity, not raw execution speed.

questions.question: Why does the enhanced for statement not hurt performance?
options: The generated code is essentially identical to the hand-written iterator or index loop.
It uses a specialized JVM instruction.
It caches all elements automatically.
It disables bounds checks entirely.
questions.explanation: The gain is readability and safety, not a special runtime optimization.

questions.question: When should you implement `Iterable` on a custom element group?
options: Whenever you want clients to use the for-each loop naturally.
Only when the type also implements `Collection`.
Only when the elements are primitive values.
Only when the type is immutable.
questions.explanation: Any type that represents a group of elements should be iterable if possible.

modules.name: 9 General Programming - Libraries and Exact Arithmetic

questions.question: Why is `Math.abs(rnd.nextInt()) % n` a bad way to generate a bounded random integer?
options: It is biased and can fail catastrophically on `Integer.MIN_VALUE`.
It is too slow for small `n`.
It always returns negative values.
It only works for prime `n`.
questions.explanation: Item 59 explicitly calls out bias and the `Integer.MIN_VALUE` edge case.

questions.question: What is the preferred modern random generator for most uses?
options: `ThreadLocalRandom`.
`Random`.
`Math.random()`.
`SplittableRandom` only, in all cases.
questions.explanation: The book says `ThreadLocalRandom` is the usual choice, with `SplittableRandom` for fork-join and parallel work.

questions.question: Why should you use `BigDecimal` for exact money calculations?
options: Binary floating-point cannot represent decimal fractions like `0.1` exactly.
Because `BigDecimal` is always faster than primitives.
Because `BigDecimal` automatically rounds to cents.
Because `double` is always imprecise for all numbers.
questions.explanation: The issue is exact decimal representation and controlled rounding, not general numeric validity.

questions.question: When is `int` or `long` a reasonable alternative to `BigDecimal`?
options: When the quantity can be represented as a scaled integer within the range you need.
Whenever exact answers are not required.
Only for scientific calculations.
Never; primitives are always unsafe.
questions.explanation: Scaled integers are a deliberate exact-arithmetic strategy when the magnitude is bounded.

questions.question: What should you do if a standard library almost solves the problem but misses a feature?
options: Check for a high-quality third-party library before implementing it yourself.
Rewrite the entire standard library function.
Use reflection to patch the library class.
Ignore the gap and duplicate the missing piece in every project.
questions.explanation: The book's order is: platform library, then third-party library, then custom code.

modules.name: 9 General Programming - Primitives, Strings, and Interfaces

questions.question: Why is `Long sum = 0L` a problem in a tight accumulation loop?
options: It forces repeated boxing and unboxing and can be much slower than `long`.
It makes the sum exact when `long` would not.
It prevents the loop from compiling.
It disables integer overflow.
questions.explanation: The issue is unnecessary object creation and auto-unboxing overhead.

questions.question: When is a boxed primitive appropriate?
options: In collections, type parameters, and other places where a reference type is required.
Whenever the value might be `null`.
Whenever a method has more than one parameter.
Whenever you want better performance.
questions.explanation: Boxed primitives are sometimes necessary, but only because the type system requires references there.

questions.question: Why are strings a poor substitute for aggregate types?
options: They hide structure, require parsing, and cannot express invariants directly.
They cannot be compared for equality.
They are always slower than arrays.
They are mutable by default.
questions.explanation: A dedicated type can expose the fields and contracts explicitly instead of encoding them in delimiters.

questions.question: What is the main reason to prefer an interface type for a variable or field?
options: It keeps the implementation swappable without changing client code.
It makes the code shorter.
It guarantees iteration order.
It forces the use of factory methods.
questions.explanation: The point is decoupling from a concrete implementation choice.

questions.question: When is it acceptable to refer to an object by its concrete class?
options: When no suitable interface exists or when class-specific behavior is required.
Whenever the class is public.
Whenever you want better compile times.
Whenever the class is final.
questions.explanation: The concrete class is appropriate only when the interface abstraction would be lossy or unavailable.

modules.name: 9 General Programming - Reflection, Native Code, Optimization, and Naming

questions.question: What is the main technical downside of reflection?
options: It bypasses compile-time type checking and is slower and more verbose than normal calls.
It cannot construct objects.
It only works for private members.
It makes code immutable.
questions.explanation: Reflection is useful, but it shifts correctness checks from compile time to runtime.

questions.question: What is the preferred way to use a class found reflectively?
options: Instantiate it reflectively, then interact with it through a known interface or superclass.
Invoke all methods reflectively forever.
Cast it to `Object` and inspect fields manually.
Avoid constructors and use native code instead.
questions.explanation: This keeps the reflective cost at the boundary and the rest of the code normal.

questions.question: When are native methods justified?
options: For platform-specific facilities or truly necessary native libraries, not as a default performance trick.
Whenever Java code allocates objects.
Whenever reflection feels verbose.
Only in test code.
questions.explanation: Native code is a narrow interoperability tool, not a general optimization strategy.

questions.question: What is the correct first step in optimization?
options: Measure with a profiler or `jmh` and identify the real hot spots.
Replace all strings with `StringBuilder`.
Inline every method.
Convert everything to native code.
questions.explanation: The book is explicit: don't guess, measure, then fix the right problem.

questions.question: Which naming rule is most conventional for a constant field?
options: Uppercase words separated by underscores.
lowerCamelCase.
PascalCase.
A single lowercase word.
questions.explanation: Constant fields are the standard exception to the normal member-name casing rules.

modules.name: 10 Exceptions - Exceptional conditions and throwable choice

questions.question: Why should exceptions not be used for ordinary loop control?
options: Because the condition is expected and should be expressed directly
Because exceptions cannot be caught inside loops
Because loop control requires checked exceptions
Because iteration with exceptions is always faster
questions.explanation: An ordinary loop should spell out its termination condition; exceptions are for genuinely unusual failure.

questions.question: When is a checked exception justified?
options: When the caller can reasonably recover and should be forced to handle the condition
Whenever the method is public
Whenever the code is performance-critical
Whenever a return value would be inconvenient
questions.explanation: The checked/unchecked choice is about recoverability and caller burden, not style.

questions.question: What should you throw for a programming error such as an invalid argument or illegal state?
options: A runtime exception appropriate to the violation
A checked exception so callers must catch it
An Error subclass
A Throwable subclass unrelated to Exception
questions.explanation: Runtime exceptions model contract violations; checked exceptions model recoverable conditions.

questions.question: What is wrong with defining a new Error subclass for application failure?
options: Errors are reserved for unrecoverable VM-level conditions and should not be part of normal API design
Errors cannot carry detail messages
Errors are always checked
Errors cannot be thrown from Java code
questions.explanation: Application code should not invent new errors; it should use runtime exceptions or checked exceptions as appropriate.

questions.question: What should a checked exception provide if recovery is expected?
options: Typed accessors that expose the data needed to recover
Only a stack trace
A localized user message
A private constructor
questions.explanation: Callers should not need to parse exception text to recover from a known failure mode.

modules.name: 10 Exceptions - Checked exceptions, standard exceptions, and abstraction

questions.question: Why can a checked exception make an API unpleasant to use?
options: Because every caller must catch it or declare it, even when recovery is awkward
Because checked exceptions are always slower at runtime
Because checked exceptions cannot be documented
Because they cannot be translated
questions.explanation: A checked exception is a deliberate API burden, so it should be reserved for useful recovery scenarios.

questions.question: When should `Optional` be preferred over a checked exception?
options: When the failure can be represented as absence and no extra failure data is needed
When the caller needs a stack trace
When the method is private
When the exception is recoverable
questions.explanation: Optional is a cleaner replacement when the failure mode is simply “no result.”

questions.question: Which exception best matches an illegal argument value?
options: `IllegalArgumentException`
`IllegalStateException`
`ConcurrentModificationException`
`UnsupportedOperationException`
questions.explanation: Bad parameter values should map to `IllegalArgumentException` unless a more specific standard exception applies.

questions.question: What is the purpose of exception translation?
options: To convert a low-level failure into an exception that matches the higher-level abstraction
To hide all failures from callers
To avoid using stack traces
To force all exceptions to become checked
questions.explanation: Translation keeps implementation details out of the public contract while preserving useful diagnostics.

questions.question: Why is chaining useful in translated exceptions?
options: It preserves the original cause and its stack trace for debugging
It eliminates the need for documentation
It makes the exception unchecked
It guarantees recovery
questions.explanation: Chaining gives you the higher-level exception the API needs plus the lower-level cause you may need later.

modules.name: 10 Exceptions - Documentation, diagnostics, and failure atomicity

questions.question: What is the best way to document a checked exception?
options: With a precise `throws` declaration and a Javadoc `@throws` description of when it occurs
Only in the class-level comment
Only by naming the exception type well
By omitting it so callers discover it from stack traces
questions.explanation: The declaration and the Javadoc should tell clients exactly when the exception is expected.

questions.question: What belongs in an exception detail message?
options: The values that explain the failure, such as offending indexes or bounds
A long prose narrative for end users
Sensitive secrets so support can diagnose faster
Only the exception class name
questions.explanation: The detail message should capture the failure facts, not tell a story.

questions.question: What is failure atomicity?
options: A failed operation leaves the object in the same usable state it had before the call
A method never throws exceptions
A method always rolls back the heap
A catch block always repairs every invariant automatically
questions.explanation: Failure atomicity is about preserving object state when an operation aborts.

questions.question: When is it acceptable to ignore an exception?
options: Only when the code comments explicitly explain why ignoring it is safe or expected
Whenever the exception is checked
Whenever the catch block is empty but short
Whenever the program is not in debug mode
questions.explanation: Ignoring an exception without explanation is a silent failure mode.

questions.question: Why can swallowing an exception make debugging harder?
options: It can defer the visible failure to a later location that no longer obviously relates to the root cause
It forces the VM to crash immediately
It always improves throughput
It prevents stack traces from being generated
questions.explanation: An ignored exception removes the evidence that would otherwise point at the original fault.

modules.name: 11 Concurrency - Synchronize shared mutable state

questions.question: Why is `count++` unsafe when multiple threads share the same counter?
options: It is a read-modify-write sequence, so updates can be lost without synchronization.
Because `int` values cannot be shared between threads.
Because `++` is always compiled as a loop.
Because `count` becomes immutable after the first increment.
questions.explanation: Compound operations need mutual exclusion or an atomic class; otherwise two threads can overwrite each other's work.

questions.question: What does `volatile` guarantee, and what does it not guarantee?
options: It guarantees visibility of writes, but not mutual exclusion or atomicity of compound actions.
It guarantees both mutual exclusion and atomicity.
It makes every method in the class thread-safe automatically.
It prevents all reordering everywhere in the JVM.
questions.explanation: A volatile field can still be involved in a race if you perform compound updates.

questions.question: What is the real target of synchronization in a mutable class?
options: The object invariant and the visibility of state transitions, not just individual fields.
Only the public methods.
Only the constructor.
Only the fields marked private.
questions.explanation: Synchronizing the invariant boundary is what keeps readers from seeing a half-updated object.

questions.question: When is a shared object usually safe without external synchronization?
options: When it is immutable and safely published, or when it is internally thread-safe by design.
Whenever it has only one mutable field.
Whenever its methods are `final`.
Whenever the code passes all unit tests on one machine.
questions.explanation: Thread safety comes from a documented policy and the memory-ordering guarantees behind it.

modules.name: 11 Concurrency - Avoid excessive synchronization

questions.question: Why are open calls dangerous inside synchronized methods?
options: Because foreign or overridable code can run while your lock is held, causing deadlock or invariant violations.
Because they prevent the JVM from compiling the method.
Because they automatically make the method recursive.
Because synchronized blocks cannot contain method calls.
questions.explanation: Calling out while holding a lock gives control to code you do not own, which is exactly where lock-order bugs appear.

questions.question: What is the best default when a synchronized region is larger than it needs to be?
options: Shrink it to the minimal section required to preserve the invariant.
Add more synchronized methods to compensate.
Replace the lock with `volatile`.
Move the lock to the caller automatically.
questions.explanation: Excessive synchronization hurts correctness and performance, so minimize the locked scope rather than masking the design flaw.

questions.question: Why can inheritance make synchronization fragile?
options: Because overridden methods may run under a lock that the subclass does not control.
Because inheritance disables thread safety completely.
Because subclasses cannot access synchronized methods.
Because locks are inherited but monitors are not.
questions.explanation: A superclass cannot safely assume what subclass code will do if it invokes overridable methods while locked.

questions.question: What is the practical benefit of copying state before calling foreign code?
options: It lets you release the lock before the callback executes.
It turns the method into a nonblocking algorithm automatically.
It makes the object immutable.
It removes the need for thread-safety documentation.
questions.explanation: A local copy narrows the critical section and avoids holding a lock across unpredictable code.

modules.name: 11 Concurrency - Executors, utilities, and documentation

questions.question: Why does Effective Java prefer executors over manually managed threads?
options: Executors separate task submission from execution policy and make pooling and scheduling explicit.
Because threads are illegal in modern Java.
Because executors always outperform threads in every workload.
Because executor tasks cannot block.
questions.explanation: An executor gives you a higher-level abstraction with fewer error-prone details to manage yourself.

questions.question: Why are `wait` and `notify` discouraged for most application code?
options: Because higher-level concurrency utilities already encode the coordination protocol more safely.
Because they do not work on synchronized objects.
Because they are slower than every other API by definition.
Because they can only be used in daemon threads.
questions.explanation: Monitor protocols are easy to get wrong; utilities such as latches and queues reduce that risk.

questions.question: What should thread-safety documentation tell clients?
options: Whether the class is thread-safe, conditionally thread-safe, immutable, or externally synchronized, and what lock or rule governs use.
Only whether the class uses `synchronized` internally.
Only whether the class has a `main` method.
Nothing, because thread safety should be obvious from the code.
questions.explanation: The thread-safety contract belongs in the API docs, not in guesswork.

questions.question: When is lazy initialization usually justified?
options: When construction is expensive or may never be needed, and the lazy path can be implemented safely.
Whenever a field is private.
Whenever you want to avoid writing a constructor.
Whenever the class is public.
questions.explanation: Lazy init is a tradeoff, not a default. Simpler eager initialization is often the better design.

modules.name: 12 Serialization - Avoid Java serialization unless you are forced into it

questions.question: What is the safest default stance toward Java serialization in a new system?
options: Avoid it and use a cross-platform structured-data format instead
Use it for every boundary because it is built into the JDK
Use it only with public fields
Use it only if the class has a serialVersionUID
questions.explanation: Item 85 says to prefer alternatives such as JSON or protobuf.

questions.question: Why is Java deserialization dangerous even when the code does not look obviously broken?
options: It can invoke code in many reachable classes during object reconstruction
It automatically encrypts the payload
It only works for final classes
It refuses to run if the stream is short
questions.explanation: The class path itself becomes part of the attack surface.

questions.question: What is a deserialization bomb?
options: A small stream that expands into very expensive work or deep recursion during deserialization
A stream that always throws `ClassNotFoundException`
A stream that only works on arrays
A stream that can only be produced by `ObjectOutputStream` in debug mode
questions.explanation: The danger is denial of service, not necessarily gadget-based code execution.

questions.question: Why is whitelisting preferred over blacklisting when filtering input streams?
options: Because blacklists only block known bad cases, while whitelists start from an explicit safe set
Because blacklists are slower in every JVM
Because whitelists always prevent bombs
Because blacklists only work with enums
questions.explanation: Item 85 treats whitelist-first filtering as the more defensible policy.

modules.name: 12 Serialization - Serializable is an exported API, not a private implementation detail

questions.question: Why does implementing `Serializable` affect future class evolution?
options: Because the serialized form becomes part of the class's exported API
Because it prevents adding methods
Because it disables generics
Because it forces all fields to be public
questions.explanation: The serialized form must remain compatible with deployed data.

questions.question: What is the main practical reason to declare an explicit `serialVersionUID`?
options: To control compatibility instead of relying on a runtime-generated hash
To make serialization encrypt the stream
To force all subclasses to share the same stream
To make `readResolve` unnecessary
questions.explanation: An explicit value avoids accidental incompatibility from unrelated source changes.

questions.question: Why does the book call deserialization a hidden constructor?
options: Because object creation happens without an ordinary constructor call and still must establish invariants
Because it always invokes the no-arg constructor first
Because it only works for records
Because it can never fail
questions.explanation: The point is that the class must defend itself even though construction is extralinguistic.

questions.question: Which kinds of classes should rarely implement `Serializable`?
options: Extendable classes and active entities such as thread pools
Only final value objects
Only nested classes
Only enums
questions.explanation: Item 86 says inheritance makes serialization much harder to support safely.

modules.name: 12 Serialization - Design the serialized form, then defend readObject

questions.question: When is the default serialized form usually acceptable?
options: When the physical representation is already a good description of the logical state
Whenever the class has many methods
Only when the class is final
Only when the class has no constructors
questions.explanation: Item 87 says the default form is fine only if it matches the logical data closely.

questions.question: Why should derived or JVM-specific fields usually be declared `transient`?
options: They are not part of the logical serialized state and should not be persisted
They make `Serializable` compile faster
They are automatically encrypted otherwise
They are required for `readResolve`
questions.explanation: Transient fields should not leak implementation details into the stream.

questions.question: Why must `defaultWriteObject` and `defaultReadObject` still be called in a custom form?
options: To preserve compatibility if nontransient fields are added later
Because the JVM rejects custom forms without them
Because they serialize transient fields too
Because they make the stream human-readable
questions.explanation: Skipping them breaks backward and forward compatibility expectations.

questions.question: What is the most important rule for synchronization inside `writeObject`?
options: It must follow the same lock-ordering and whole-object consistency rules as other methods
It should never use a lock
It must always be static
It should only synchronize on `this` if the class is immutable
questions.explanation: Serialization must respect the class's concurrency discipline.

modules.name: 12 Serialization - Defensive deserialization, readResolve, and serialization proxies

questions.question: Why must `readObject` behave like a constructor?
options: Because it creates an instance from attacker-controlled bytes and must establish all invariants
Because it is always invoked before fields are allocated
Because it can skip validation if `Serializable` is present
Because it only runs for enums
questions.explanation: Item 88 says to validate and copy just as a real constructor would.

questions.question: What is the point of defensively copying mutable components during `readObject`?
options: To prevent outsiders from retaining references to private mutable state
To reduce the number of serialized fields
To make the stream shorter
To avoid `defaultReadObject`
questions.explanation: Without the copy, an attacker can keep aliases to the object's internals.

questions.question: Why is an enum the preferred tool for a serializable singleton?
options: It gives the JVM a built-in instance-control guarantee that `readResolve` cannot match as safely
It automatically makes all fields transient
It removes the need for constructors in every class
It allows any number of runtime instances
questions.explanation: Item 89 recommends enums over fragile `readResolve` tricks whenever possible.

questions.question: What is the key benefit of the serialization proxy pattern?
options: It reconstructs the object through the public API instead of exposing raw internal state
It makes the object serializable without `Serializable`
It eliminates the need for a nested class
It works best only for extendable classes
questions.explanation: The proxy shifts deserialization back into ordinary, validated object construction.
